title: The Basic CNN Recipe
concept: A classic CNN usually repeats convolution, activation, and pooling in stages, then uses a classifier head to turn the final features into class scores.

exercises.prompt: Recap: After a convolution finds useful patterns, what is pooling usually doing next?

options.text: Shrinking the feature map while keeping the strongest summary of what was found
Turning the image back into raw RGB pixels
Replacing every learned feature with a random number

exercises.explain: Exactly. Pooling usually compresses a feature map after useful patterns have already been detected.

cards.title: A CNN is usually built from a recipe
cards.body: A beginner can look at a CNN and see a scary wall of layers. But many classic CNNs follow a simple recipe: find patterns, apply a nonlinearity, shrink the map a little, then repeat. Only near the end does the network make its final class decision.

cards.title: The basic CNN recipe
cards.body: A common CNN stage is convolution -> activation -> pooling. The convolution learns patterns, the activation keeps the model flexible and nonlinear, and the pooling step shrinks the feature map so the next stage can build on a more compact summary. After several such stages, a classifier head turns the final features into class scores.

cards.title: Cook, taste, reduce — then plate
cards.body: Imagine making a sauce. First you add ingredients, then the heat changes them, then you reduce the sauce so the flavor becomes more concentrated. A CNN does something similar: convolution adds learned pattern detectors, activation reshapes the response, and pooling reduces the map so the next stage works with a stronger summary.

cards.title: A toy CNN from image to logits
cards.body: Picture a tiny 3-class image model: 32x32x3 -> Conv(8) -> ReLU -> MaxPool -> 16x16x8 -> Conv(16) -> ReLU -> MaxPool -> 8x8x16 -> Flatten -> Dense -> 3 logits. The first stage can notice simple edges. The second stage can combine those edges into bigger shapes. The dense layer at the end reads the final feature stack and produces one score per class.

cards.title: Pooling is not the whole brain
cards.body: Pooling matters, but it is not the part learning the visual patterns in the first place. The convolution layers do the main feature-finding work. Pooling mostly helps summarize and shrink those learned responses so the network can go deeper without carrying a giant map forever.

cards.title: Repeated stages vs. final head
cards.body: A basic CNN has two jobs: build features, then make a decision.

left.text:
Conv finds patterns
Activation reshapes responses
Pooling shrinks the map

right.text:
Flatten or global pool
Dense layer reads final features
Outputs class logits

exercises.prompt: Which sequence best matches the classic beginner-friendly CNN recipe?

options.text: Conv -> ReLU -> Pool, repeated for a few stages, then a classifier head
Classifier head first, feature extraction later

exercises.explain: Right — classic CNNs usually build features in repeated stages before the final head makes the class decision.

exercises.prompt: True or false: In a basic CNN, the classifier head usually comes after several feature-extraction stages, not right after the very first convolution.
exercises.explain: True — early stages build useful features first, and the head reads those later features to make the final prediction.

exercises.sentence: A classic CNN often repeats conv -> activation -> ___ before the final classifier head.
exercises.explain: Pooling — that shrinking step is the common third part of the classic recipe.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best captures what the repeated middle of a CNN is doing?

options.text: It keeps turning raw pixels into richer and more compact learned features, stage by stage
It keeps resetting the network back to the original image each time
It mainly exists to remove all learned structure before classification

exercises.explain: Exactly — the repeated stages gradually turn pixels into feature maps that are easier for the head to classify.

exercises.prompt: You are building a toy CNN for classifying handwritten digits from 28x28 images. Which layout makes the most architectural sense?

options.text: Conv -> ReLU -> Pool -> Conv -> ReLU -> Pool -> Flatten -> Dense -> 10 logits
Pool -> Pool -> Pool -> output label with no learned filters
Flatten the raw image immediately and skip feature extraction entirely, because CNNs do not need spatial stages

exercises.explain: Exactly. A basic CNN usually extracts features in repeated conv/activation/pooling stages, then uses a head at the end to output class scores. Next we'll zoom in on a key design idea: CNNs are often organized as blocks, not just isolated layers.
